TMS WM Analysis

2025-1-29

Packages

# import packages
library(lme4)     # mixed models
## Warning: package 'lme4' was built under R version 4.3.2
## Loading required package: Matrix
library(dplyr)    # dataframe manipulation
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(readr)    # reading in the data
library(emmeans)  # likelihood ratio testing
## Warning: package 'emmeans' was built under R version 4.3.3
## Welcome to emmeans.
## Caution: You lose important information if you filter this package's results.
## See '? untidy'
library(purrr)    # for map()
library(lattice)  # for xyplot()
library(TAF)      # setting up output file directory structure during model fitting
library(moments)  # skewness for selecting between additive and multiplicative models of response times
## Warning: package 'moments' was built under R version 4.3.3
library(stringr)
library(filelock)
## Warning: package 'filelock' was built under R version 4.3.3
#If you get an error, you likely need to install the packages using the "install.package('insert-package-name-here')" command

Notebook configurable settings

# name the directories to read data from and write output to
input_version = 9
output_version = 'programming'
program_type = 'short'

make an overall output directory (model summaries, stats, etc. will all go here)

#setwd("..")
outputdir = sprintf("%s/../candidate-models/regression-models-%s", getwd(), output_version)
mkdir(outputdir)

Loading Data

#setwd("..")
input_file = sprintf("%s/../preprocessed-data/full-data-version-%d/programming-full-data.csv", getwd(), input_version)
data <- read.csv(input_file)

#Transforming the session number such that it is treated as a character and not a value
data <- transform( 
  data, session = as.character(session)) 

#Transforming correctness into binaries of 1 or 0
data$correctness <- as.integer(ifelse(data$accuracy == 'Pass', 1, 0))

Removing irrelevant data from dataframe

#remove rows that have no values - nothing actually happens here. Everyone saw the stimuli
data <- data[data$duration > 1, ] 


#Removing the one participants that didn't listen to instructions (2 SD below the mean in N-back):
participants_to_remove <- c("REDACTED_20")
data <- subset(data, !(participant_id %in% participants_to_remove))

Looking at FITB and the passed ones only - Make sure there are 533 rows

data <- data[data$problem_type == program_type, ]


number_of_rows <- nrow(data)
print(number_of_rows) #number of rows
## [1] 533

Make sure you have n=27 participants

#Make sure you have n=27 participants

id <- unique(data$participant_id)
id #all unique IDs
##  [1] "REDACTED_0"  "REDACTED_1"  "REDACTED_10" "REDACTED_11" "REDACTED_12"
##  [6] "REDACTED_13" "REDACTED_14" "REDACTED_15" "REDACTED_16" "REDACTED_17"
## [11] "REDACTED_18" "REDACTED_19" "REDACTED_2"  "REDACTED_21" "REDACTED_22"
## [16] "REDACTED_23" "REDACTED_24" "REDACTED_25" "REDACTED_26" "REDACTED_27"
## [21] "REDACTED_3"  "REDACTED_4"  "REDACTED_5"  "REDACTED_6"  "REDACTED_7" 
## [26] "REDACTED_8"  "REDACTED_9"
length(id) #number of participants
## [1] 27

Mean correctness

#Make sure you have n=27 participants

mean_value <- mean((data$correctness))
std_value <- sd((data$correctness))
print(mean_value)
## [1] 0.6904315
print(std_value)
## [1] 0.46275

Mean corectness is 0.69 with a standard deviation of 0.46

Table of problems views by participants. Some participants saw only 7-8 problems per session while some saw 11-12 per session.

count_table <- table(data$TMS_Type, data$participant_id)

print(count_table)
##     
##      REDACTED_0 REDACTED_1 REDACTED_10 REDACTED_11 REDACTED_12 REDACTED_13
##   CM         12          8           8          12           8           8
##   WM         12          8           8          12           8           8
##     
##      REDACTED_14 REDACTED_15 REDACTED_16 REDACTED_17 REDACTED_18 REDACTED_19
##   CM          12          12          11           8          12          12
##   WM          12          12          12           8          12          11
##     
##      REDACTED_2 REDACTED_21 REDACTED_22 REDACTED_23 REDACTED_24 REDACTED_25
##   CM          8          12           8           8           8           8
##   WM          8          12           8           8           8           8
##     
##      REDACTED_26 REDACTED_27 REDACTED_3 REDACTED_4 REDACTED_5 REDACTED_6
##   CM          12          12         12          8         12          8
##   WM          12          12         12          8         12          8
##     
##      REDACTED_7 REDACTED_8 REDACTED_9
##   CM          8         12          7
##   WM          8         12          8

Select independent variables

  1. participant_id (person) : random intercept

    • People are drawn from an underlying distribution; we aren’t interested in learning coefficients specific to the members of our sample, but rather in what they tell us about that underlying population. Random effects tell us about the distribution rather than the elements observed.

    • This variable is used to model person-specific attributes, such as a “skill,” translating to overall faster or slower response times, that can be different for each person

  2. problem_id (question prompt) : random intercept
    • The underlying distribution of stimuli is a little less clear than that of participants. This variable can be thought of as modeling the dispersion of question-specific difficulties.
  3. TMS_Type (TMS treatment condition, i.e. brain region stimulated) : fixed & random intercepts
    • Based on previous literature, we hypothesize that the brain region stimulated may have an effect that is systematic (i.e. very consistent between people, topics, etc.), quantitatively heterogeneous (i.e. directionally consistent, but widely varying in magnitude), or even a qualitatively heterogeneous (i.e. varying in direction, possibly in conjunction with magnitude). Therefore, we assess the goodness of fit for candidate models containing TMS_Type as a fixed effect, random effect, both, or neither, in order to test this hypothesis.
  4. sessionand coding_section_no (chronological numbering of the session, per participant) : fixed intercept
    • This is used to account for learning/training effect, i.e. participants getting better at test-taking under our specific test instrument over the course of the study. For example, they may become more familiar with the question structure, or become better at recalling old material as they do more practice questions. We expect this to be systematic (uniformly decreasing response time), so we model it as a fixed effect.

Specify candidate predictor structures for mixed model formulas

fixed_effects = c(
  # no fixed effects
  "", 
  
  # no interactions
  "TMS_Type", 
  "session", 
  "coding_section_no", 
  "problem_order", 
  "problem_set", 
  
  "pain",
  "physical_symptoms",
  "discomfort", 
  "sensations",
  
  "TMS_Type + session",
  "TMS_Type + coding_section_no",
  "TMS_Type + problem_order",
  "TMS_Type + problem_set",
  "session + coding_section_no",
  "session + problem_order",
  "session + problem_set",
  "problem_set + coding_section_no",
  "problem_set + problem_order",
  "coding_section_no + problem_order",
  
  "TMS_Type + pain",
  "TMS_Type + physical_symptoms",
  "TMS_Type + discomfort",
  "TMS_Type + sensations",
  "session + pain",
  "session + physical_symptoms",
  "session + discomfort",
  "session + sensations",
  "coding_section_no + pain",
  "coding_section_no + physical_symptoms",
  "coding_section_no + discomfort",
  "coding_section_no + sensations",
  "problem_order + pain",
  "problem_order + physical_symptoms",
  "problem_order + discomfort",
  "problem_order + sensations",
  "problem_set + pain",
  "problem_set + physical_symptoms",
  "problem_set + discomfort",
  "problem_set + sensations",
  
  "TMS_Type + session + coding_section_no + problem_order + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  
  # 2-way interactions
  "TMS_Type * session",
  "TMS_Type * coding_section_no",
  "TMS_Type * problem_order",
  "TMS_Type * problem_set",
  "session * coding_section_no",
  "session * problem_order",
  "session * problem_set",
  "problem_set * coding_section_no",
  "problem_set * problem_order",
  "coding_section_no * problem_order",

  "TMS_Type * pain",
  "TMS_Type * physical_symptoms",
  "TMS_Type * discomfort",
  "TMS_Type * sensations", 
  "session * pain",
  "session * physical_symptoms",
  "session * discomfort",
  "session * sensations",
  "coding_section_no * pain",
  "coding_section_no * physical_symptoms",
  "coding_section_no * discomfort",
  "coding_section_no * sensations",
  "problem_order * pain",
  "problem_order * physical_symptoms",
  "problem_order * discomfort",
  "problem_order * sensations",
  "problem_set * pain",
  "problem_set * physical_symptoms",
  "problem_set * discomfort",
  "problem_set * sensations",
  
  # 1- and 2-way interactions
  "TMS_Type * session + problem_set + coding_section_no + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_set + session + coding_section_no + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * coding_section_no + session + problem_set + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_order + session + problem_set + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "session * problem_set + TMS_Type + coding_section_no + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "session * coding_section_no + TMS_Type + problem_set + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "session * problem_order + TMS_Type + problem_set + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "problem_set * coding_section_no + TMS_Type + session + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "problem_set * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  
  "TMS_Type * pain + session + problem_set + coding_section_no + problem_order",
  "TMS_Type * physical_symptoms + session + problem_set + coding_section_no + problem_order",
  "TMS_Type * discomfort + session + problem_set + coding_section_no + problem_order",
  "TMS_Type * sensations + session + problem_set + coding_section_no + problem_order", 
  
  # multiple 2-way interactions
  "TMS_Type * session + problem_set * coding_section_no + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * session + problem_set * problem_order + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * session + coding_section_no * problem_order + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_set + session * coding_section_no + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_set + session * problem_order + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_set + coding_section_no * problem_order + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * coding_section_no + session * problem_set + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * coding_section_no + session * problem_order + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * coding_section_no + problem_set * problem_order + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_order + session * problem_set + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_order + session * coding_section_no + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "TMS_Type * problem_order + problem_set * coding_section_no + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "session * problem_set + coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "session * coding_section_no + problem_set * problem_order + TMS_Type + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  "session * problem_order + problem_set * coding_section_no + TMS_Type + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations",
  
  # 3-way interactions
  "TMS_Type * session * problem_set + coding_section_no + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "TMS_Type * session * coding_section_no + problem_set + problem_order + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "TMS_Type * session * problem_order + problem_set + coding_section_no + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "TMS_Type * problem_set * coding_section_no + problem_order + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "TMS_Type * problem_set * problem_order + coding_section_no + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "TMS_Type * coding_section_no * problem_order + session + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "session * problem_set * coding_section_no + problem_order + TMS_Type + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "session * problem_set * problem_order + coding_section_no + TMS_Type + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "session * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + problem_set + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations", 
  "problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations"
)
random_effects = c(
  #"",                               # (1)
  "participant_id", 
  "problem_id", 
  #"domain",                         # (2)
  #"condition",                      # (2)
  #"id:stimulus",                    # (3)
  #"participant_id:problem_type",
  "participant_id:TMS_Type", 
  #"stimulus:domain",                # (4)
  "problem_id:TMS_Type" #, 
  #"domain:condition",               # (2)
  #"id:stimulus:domain",             # (3), (4)
  #"id:stimulus:condition",          # (3)
  #"participant_id:problem_type:TMS_Type"
  #"stimulus:domain:condition",      # (2), (4)
  #"id:stimulus:domain:condition"    # (2), (3), (4)
)

In this first cell, we enumerate random effect structures by taking all combinations of the random effects specified.

random_effects = map(random_effects, function (s) sprintf("(1|%s)", s))

random_exprs = c()
N_random = length(random_effects)
for (k in 1:N_random) {
  iii = combn(1:N_random, k, simplify=FALSE)
  for (ii in iii) {
    expr = paste(random_effects[ii], collapse = " + ")
    random_exprs = append(random_exprs, expr)
  }
}

Now, we combine the response variable, fixed effects, and random effects together to generate our candidate model structures.

logistic_formulae = c()
for (fe in fixed_effects) {
  for (re in random_exprs) {
    f = ""
    if (fe == "") {
      f = sprintf("correctness ~ %s", re)
    } else {
      f = sprintf("correctness ~ %s + %s", fe, re)
    }
    logistic_formulae = append(logistic_formulae, f)
  }
}

print(length(logistic_formulae))
## [1] 1650

Filter out invalid model specifications

# generate all the boolean index vectors

# formulas with TMS fixed effect
has_TMS <- grepl('TMS_Type ', logistic_formulae, fixed = TRUE)

# formulas with participant random effect
has_participant <- grepl('(1|participant_id)', logistic_formulae, fixed = TRUE)

# formulas with problem random effect
has_problem <- grepl('(1|problem_id)', logistic_formulae, fixed = TRUE)

# formulas with TMS x participant random effect
has_TMS_participant <- grepl('(1|participant_id:TMS_Type)', logistic_formulae, fixed = TRUE)

# formulas with TMS x problem random effect
has_TMS_problem <- grepl('(1|problem_id:TMS_Type)', logistic_formulae, fixed = TRUE)

# remove them

invalid <- (has_TMS_participant & (!has_TMS | !has_participant)) | (has_TMS_problem & (!has_TMS | !has_problem))

invalid_logistic_formulae <- logistic_formulae[invalid]
logistic_formulae <- logistic_formulae[!invalid]

print(length(logistic_formulae))
## [1] 615

Model fitting (~2 minutes)

Here, we fit all the models. Of note, we use MLE rather than REML for compatibility with the AIC model selection strategy.

We also store BIC (an alternative model selection criterion) in addition to AIC here. The two have different theoretical properties, both of which may be useful. Both describe the log likelihood of a fitted model, with a penalty for model complexity. The penalty in BIC is higher than that for AIC, so optimal-BIC models tend to be smaller (with fewer parameters) than optimal-AIC models. We have done our analysis relying on AIC, but we provide BIC-based model selection here in case the reader is interested.

# make an output directory for the output of all the candidate linear models
correctness_outputdir = 
  sprintf("%s/FITB_v9-2_acc_model_summaries", outputdir)
mkdir(correctness_outputdir)
if (TRUE) {
  # make an output file for the AIC and BIC of each model
  metricsfile = sprintf("%s/metrics.csv", correctness_outputdir)
  file.create(metricsfile)
  
  sink(file = metricsfile,
       append = FALSE,
       type = "output",
       split = FALSE
       )
  
  cat("formula,AIC,BIC\n")
  
  sink()
  
  for (f in logistic_formulae) {
    # make an output directory for the output of this specific candidate model
    f_outputdir = sprintf("%s/%s", correctness_outputdir, gsub(" ", "", f, fixed = TRUE))
    mkdir(f_outputdir)
    
    # make the individual output file
    summaryfile = sprintf("%s/summary.txt", f_outputdir)
    
    file.create(summaryfile)
    
    # print a delimiter for console output
    cat(sprintf("\n---------------------------------------------\n%s\n", f))
    
    # fit the model
    #m = try(glmer(f, family=binomial, data=data))
    
    result <- tryCatch({
      # Code that may produce an error
      m = try(glmer(f, family=binomial, data=data))
      # send the model summary to the summary file
      sink(file = summaryfile,
         append = FALSE,
         type = "output",
         split = FALSE
         )
    
      print(summary(m))
    
      sink()
      
      # send the AIC and BIC to the metrics file
      metrics = sprintf("%s,%f,%f\n", f, AIC(m), BIC(m))
    
      sink(file = metricsfile,
         append = TRUE,
         type = "output",
         split = FALSE)
    
      cat(metrics)
    
      sink()
      
      }, error = function(e) {
      # Handle the error
      cat("An error occurred:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
      NA
      })
    
  }
}
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ (1|participant_id)
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ (1|problem_id)
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id)

## correctness ~ TMS_Type + problem_order + (1|participant_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type)
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ TMS_Type + problem_order + (1|problem_id) + (1|problem_id:TMS_Type)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')

## correctness ~ TMS_Type + problem_order + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type)

## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|participant_id)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.00751258 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|problem_id)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0032513 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0317172 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|participant_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0120239 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|problem_id) + (1|problem_id:TMS_Type)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0141593 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0205433 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|problem_id:TMS_Type)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0108092 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it
## 
## ---------------------------------------------
## correctness ~ problem_set * coding_section_no * problem_order + TMS_Type + session + pain + physical_symptoms + discomfort + sensations + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type) + (1|problem_id:TMS_Type)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, :
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0178996 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 14 > 12.
## Use print(summary(m), correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(summary(m))        if you need it

Model selection

# read in the AIC/BIC data
#fin = sprintf("%s/FITB_MWP_babytest_model_summaries/metrics.csv", outputdir) #from original submission
fin = sprintf("%s/FITB_v9-2_acc_model_summaries/metrics.csv", outputdir)
correctness_models_da = read_csv(fin)
## Rows: 615 Columns: 3
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): formula
## dbl (2): AIC, BIC
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
dim(correctness_models_da)
## [1] 615   3
head(correctness_models_da[order(correctness_models_da$AIC),], n = 10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 3
##    formula                                                             AIC   BIC
##    <chr>                                                             <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) …  472.  498.
##  2 correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) …  474.  504.
##  3 correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) …  474.  504.
##  4 correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) …  476.  510.
##  5 correctness ~ TMS_Type * sensations + session + problem_set + co…  477.  524.
##  6 correctness ~ TMS_Type * discomfort + session + problem_set + co…  477.  520.
##  7 correctness ~ TMS_Type * physical_symptoms + session + problem_s…  477.  520.
##  8 correctness ~ session + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) +…  478.  499.
##  9 correctness ~ problem_set * problem_order + TMS_Type + session +…  478.  538.
## 10 correctness ~ coding_section_no + sensations + (1|participant_id…  478.  499.
head(correctness_models_da[order(correctness_models_da$BIC),], n = 10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 3
##    formula                                                             AIC   BIC
##    <chr>                                                             <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|proble…  479.  496.
##  2 correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) …  472.  498.
##  3 correctness ~ session + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) +…  478.  499.
##  4 correctness ~ coding_section_no + sensations + (1|participant_id…  478.  499.
##  5 correctness ~ coding_section_no + discomfort + (1|participant_id…  479.  500.
##  6 correctness ~ coding_section_no + pain + (1|participant_id) + (1…  479.  501.
##  7 correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) …  480.  502.
##  8 correctness ~ problem_set + coding_section_no + (1|participant_i…  480.  502.
##  9 correctness ~ (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id)                  489.  502.
## 10 correctness ~ coding_section_no + problem_order + (1|participant…  481.  502.

Our selection process pulls correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type) as the best model to represent the data by AIC (and 2nd-best by BIC).

fA <- correctness_models_da[order(correctness_models_da$AIC),]$formula[[1]]
cat(fA)
## correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type)

BIC removes exactly the TMS_Type terms.

fB <- correctness_models_da[order(correctness_models_da$BIC),]$formula[[1]]
cat(fB)
## correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id)
m0 <- glmer(fA, family=binomial, data=data)
summary(m0)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) +  
##     (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##    Data: data
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    472.3    497.9   -230.1    460.3      527 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.3104 -0.1738  0.1997  0.3948  3.2388 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups                  Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  participant_id:TMS_Type (Intercept) 1.186    1.0888  
##  participant_id          (Intercept) 0.768    0.8764  
##  problem_id              (Intercept) 6.214    2.4928  
## Number of obs: 533, groups:  
## participant_id:TMS_Type, 54; participant_id, 27; problem_id, 24
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                   Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)        0.07388    0.70897   0.104 0.917000    
## TMS_TypeWM        -0.22150    0.41829  -0.530 0.596423    
## coding_section_no  0.61185    0.18536   3.301 0.000964 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr) TMS_TW
## TMS_TypeWM  -0.297       
## cdng_sctn_n -0.473 -0.003

The fixed effect is not significant. Next we check if (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type) has statistical significance.

Check whether to include the factor

m1 <- glmer(fB, family=binomial, data=data)
summary(m1)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 |  
##     problem_id)
##    Data: data
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    479.3    496.5   -235.7    471.3      529 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.5534 -0.1867  0.2139  0.4573  2.3356 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups         Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  participant_id (Intercept) 1.098    1.048   
##  problem_id     (Intercept) 5.186    2.277   
## Number of obs: 533, groups:  participant_id, 27; problem_id, 24
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                   Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)       -0.06895    0.61225  -0.113 0.910331    
## coding_section_no  0.57840    0.16611   3.482 0.000498 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## cdng_sctn_n -0.501
anova(m0, m1)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m1: correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id)
## m0: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##    npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)   
## m1    4 479.35 496.46 -235.67   471.35                        
## m0    6 472.25 497.92 -230.13   460.25 11.093  2   0.003901 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

This means including the TMS_Type variable is significant. Again though, it is not the marginal effect:

# both with interaction term
m0_tmp <- glmer(
  correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id), 
  family=binomial, 
  data=data
)
m1_tmp <- glmer(
  correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id), 
  family=binomial, 
  data=data
)
anova(m0_tmp, m1_tmp)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m1_tmp: correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id)
## m0_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id)
##        npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
## m1_tmp    4 479.35 496.46 -235.67   471.35                     
## m0_tmp    5 480.32 501.71 -235.16   470.32 1.0259  1     0.3111
# both without interaction term
m0_tmp <- glmer(
  correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type), 
  family=binomial, 
  data=data
)
m1_tmp <- glmer(
  correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type), 
  family=binomial, 
  data=data
)
anova(m0_tmp, m1_tmp)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m1_tmp: correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
## m0_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##        npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
## m1_tmp    5 470.53 491.92 -230.26   460.53                    
## m0_tmp    6 472.25 497.92 -230.13   460.25 0.277  1     0.5987

Check if random effect is statistically significant

m2 <- glmer(
  correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id), 
  family=binomial, 
  data=data
)
anova(m0, m2)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m2: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id)
## m0: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##    npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)   
## m2    5 480.32 501.71 -235.16   470.32                        
## m0    6 472.25 497.92 -230.13   460.25 10.067  1   0.001509 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

This is actually a stronger p-value than the ‘omnibus’ test removing both terms. So the random interaction intercept is significant (although the bootstrapping-based confint should be reported, rather than this p-value, because likelihood-based methods are anti-conservative for glmer modle random intercepts).

Confidence Intervals and PSVE

Point estimate confint

summary(m0)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) +  
##     (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##    Data: data
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    472.3    497.9   -230.1    460.3      527 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.3104 -0.1738  0.1997  0.3948  3.2388 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups                  Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  participant_id:TMS_Type (Intercept) 1.186    1.0888  
##  participant_id          (Intercept) 0.768    0.8764  
##  problem_id              (Intercept) 6.214    2.4928  
## Number of obs: 533, groups:  
## participant_id:TMS_Type, 54; participant_id, 27; problem_id, 24
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                   Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)        0.07388    0.70897   0.104 0.917000    
## TMS_TypeWM        -0.22150    0.41829  -0.530 0.596423    
## coding_section_no  0.61185    0.18536   3.301 0.000964 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr) TMS_TW
## TMS_TypeWM  -0.297       
## cdng_sctn_n -0.473 -0.003
pp0 = profile(m0)
confint(pp0)
##                        2.5 %    97.5 %
## .sig01             0.5376908 1.8074948
## .sig02             0.0000000 1.6099218
## .sig03             1.7167084 3.7817997
## (Intercept)       -1.3641007 1.5063538
## TMS_TypeWM        -1.0763747 0.6481248
## coding_section_no  0.2562715 0.9858146
xyplot(pp0)

\(\sigma_1\) excludes zero, so this further confirms the likelihood ratio test above.

Percentage of Structural Variance Explained

For the structural ICC statistic presented in the paper, see the separate bootstrapping scripts in arm_2_bootstrap/. This PSVE statistic is fine for looking at one experiment in isolation, but is problematic for comparing between different experiments with different stimulus variance (e.g., our two study arms, discussed in Section 4.2.3).

# Simple thread-safe append function
safe_write <- function(row, fout, lock_file) {
  lck <- lock(lock_file) # Acquire lock
  # on.exit(unlock(lck))  # Ensure unlock on finish or error

  sink(
    file = fout,
    append = TRUE,
    type = "output",
    split = FALSE
  )
  cat(row, " \n")
  sink()
  unlock(lck) # Release lock
}
# Callback function for each bootstrap simulation
get_psve <- function(m, effect, save = FALSE, fout, effect_order, f_lock) {
  # m is a glmer object so residual variance isn"t well-defined, hence "structural"
  # Store variance estimates for all random effects
  random_effects <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m)) %>%
    rename(Groups = grp, Name = var1, Variance = vcov, Std.Dev. = sdcor) %>%
    select(-var2)

  num <- (random_effects %>% filter(Groups == effect))$Variance
  denom <- sum(random_effects$Variance)

  if (save) {
    if (missing(fout) || missing(effect_order)) {
      warning(sprintf(
        paste0(
          "get_psve(m, effect=%s, save=TRUE) failed to save: ",
          "fout or effect_order not provided"
        ),
        effect
      ))
    } else {
      vv <- sapply(effect_order, function(eff) {
        (random_effects %>% filter(Groups == eff))$Variance
      }) # this is to ensure that they appear in the same order every time
      safe_write(paste0(vv, collapse = ", "), fout, f_lock)
    }
  }

  return(c(num / denom))
}

get_psve(m0, "participant_id:TMS_Type")
## [1] 0.1451535
#tmp <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m0))
#tmp$grp
VarCorr(m0)
##  Groups                  Name        Std.Dev.
##  participant_id:TMS_Type (Intercept) 1.08885 
##  participant_id          (Intercept) 0.87636 
##  problem_id              (Intercept) 2.49284

PSVE confint

Of the three significance tests here, this one (bootstrapping) is the most robust when nsim is big enough. source, source

ordered_factors <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m0))$grp
nsim <- 10000
# 10k sims took about 2.5 hrs

psve_log <- sprintf("%s/psve_bootstrap_%i.csv", correctness_outputdir, nsim)
file.create(psve_log)
## [1] TRUE
lock_file <- paste0(psve_log, ".lock") # Define a lock file
cat(psve_log, "\n")
## /data1/eshedden/umich/tms_journal/SE-TMS-2026/arm_2/data-analysis/data-analysis-scripts/../candidate-models/regression-models-programming/FITB_v9-2_acc_model_summaries/psve_bootstrap_10000.csv
# write the header
cat(paste0(ordered_factors, collapse = ","), "\n", file = psve_log)

b0 <- bootMer(
  m0,
  FUN = function(m) {
    get_psve(
      m,
      "participant_id:TMS_Type",
      save = TRUE,
      psve_log,
      ordered_factors,
      lock_file
    )
  },
  nsim = nsim,
  #.progress = "txt"
  parallel = "multicore",
  ncpus = 64
)
quantile(b0$t, c(0.025, 0.975))
##         2.5%        97.5% 
## 4.684757e-10 3.185672e-01
bootstrap_sims <- read.csv(psve_log)
bootstrap_sims$sum <- rowSums(bootstrap_sims)
bootstrap_sims$psve <- bootstrap_sims$participant_id.TMS_Type / bootstrap_sims$sum
bootstrap_sims <- bootstrap_sims %>% arrange(psve) 
bootstrap_sims %>% head(50)
##    participant_id.TMS_Type participant_id problem_id       sum         psve
## 1             0.000000e+00      0.9697046   4.218357  5.188062 0.000000e+00
## 2             0.000000e+00      1.7563570   8.003979  9.760336 0.000000e+00
## 3             0.000000e+00      0.2491450   3.666383  3.915528 0.000000e+00
## 4             0.000000e+00      0.9136201   3.577434  4.491055 0.000000e+00
## 5             0.000000e+00      2.4406112  11.680584 14.121195 0.000000e+00
## 6             0.000000e+00      0.2711874   4.758704  5.029892 0.000000e+00
## 7             0.000000e+00      1.2730507   5.323682  6.596732 0.000000e+00
## 8             0.000000e+00      1.3242959   6.567936  7.892232 0.000000e+00
## 9             0.000000e+00      3.1901985  10.268741 13.458939 0.000000e+00
## 10            0.000000e+00      1.0483765   6.329148  7.377525 0.000000e+00
## 11            0.000000e+00      0.4575165   3.096471  3.553987 0.000000e+00
## 12            0.000000e+00      3.5722192  22.266564 25.838783 0.000000e+00
## 13            0.000000e+00      1.3774474   5.506844  6.884291 0.000000e+00
## 14            0.000000e+00      0.9406444   8.908194  9.848838 0.000000e+00
## 15            0.000000e+00      1.0081276  12.984900 13.993028 0.000000e+00
## 16            0.000000e+00      1.0367632   5.194091  6.230854 0.000000e+00
## 17            0.000000e+00      1.7751076   9.326031 11.101138 0.000000e+00
## 18            0.000000e+00      0.8455438   6.250957  7.096501 0.000000e+00
## 19            0.000000e+00      1.2366489   7.356386  8.593034 0.000000e+00
## 20            0.000000e+00      1.5911806   6.973709  8.564890 0.000000e+00
## 21            0.000000e+00      1.8402910   8.287350 10.127641 0.000000e+00
## 22            0.000000e+00      2.5627662   9.191671 11.754437 0.000000e+00
## 23            0.000000e+00      1.7284329   6.007770  7.736203 0.000000e+00
## 24            0.000000e+00      0.3410129   8.784259  9.125272 0.000000e+00
## 25            0.000000e+00      1.5294463   5.782224  7.311670 0.000000e+00
## 26            0.000000e+00      0.9260355   3.345907  4.271942 0.000000e+00
## 27            0.000000e+00      1.8518806  12.041286 13.893166 0.000000e+00
## 28            0.000000e+00      3.0260347   9.765274 12.791309 0.000000e+00
## 29            0.000000e+00      0.8266484   5.055150  5.881799 0.000000e+00
## 30            0.000000e+00      1.8627423   5.191732  7.054474 0.000000e+00
## 31            0.000000e+00      0.4557224   5.110608  5.566330 0.000000e+00
## 32            0.000000e+00      1.4091497   7.492720  8.901870 0.000000e+00
## 33            0.000000e+00      0.9822559   3.566423  4.548678 0.000000e+00
## 34            0.000000e+00      1.0547223   2.803692  3.858415 0.000000e+00
## 35            0.000000e+00      2.4416355  11.122216 13.563852 0.000000e+00
## 36            0.000000e+00      0.6634657  11.401043 12.064508 0.000000e+00
## 37            0.000000e+00      1.5657481   4.217687  5.783435 0.000000e+00
## 38            0.000000e+00      1.2664639   8.993474 10.259938 0.000000e+00
## 39            0.000000e+00      1.9894532  10.802111 12.791564 0.000000e+00
## 40            0.000000e+00      0.7182280   8.768652  9.486880 0.000000e+00
## 41            0.000000e+00      1.6380120   5.633114  7.271126 0.000000e+00
## 42            1.822204e-13      1.5461903   7.482885  9.029076 2.018151e-14
## 43            7.441766e-13      1.4761701   9.810707 11.286877 6.593291e-14
## 44            9.628801e-13      1.8184176   5.393736  7.212154 1.335080e-13
## 45            2.818528e-12      1.7015078  15.766133 17.467641 1.613571e-13
## 46            8.410488e-13      0.9554359   3.252127  4.207563 1.998898e-13
## 47            2.400124e-12      2.4731430   8.921529 11.394672 2.106357e-13
## 48            2.569540e-12      1.0389026   7.412617  8.451519 3.040330e-13
## 49            2.802664e-12      0.7467622   5.427864  6.174626 4.539002e-13
## 50            7.083307e-12      1.1260047  13.634069 14.760074 4.798965e-13
quantile(bootstrap_sims$participant_id.TMS_Type, c(0.025, 0.975))
##         2.5%        97.5% 
## 3.419113e-09 2.426873e+00
quantile(bootstrap_sims$psve, c(0.025, 0.975))
##         2.5%        97.5% 
## 4.690768e-10 3.185595e-01
bottom_percentile <- 3
bottom_percentile_idx <- (nsim * bottom_percentile / 100)
# Distribution
hist(bootstrap_sims$psve, breaks=20, right=FALSE)
abline(v = quantile(b0$t, 0.025), col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 2.5 percentile
abline(v = get_psve(m0, "participant_id:TMS_Type"), col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # estimate
abline(v = quantile(b0$t, 0.975), col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 97.5 percentile

# Low-PSVE simulations
plot(bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$psve, 
     type="o", 
     main=sprintf('Simulations in bottom %d%% PSVE', 
                  bottom_percentile), 
     xlab='Simulation #', 
     ylab='PSVE')
abline(v = 0.025*nsim, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 2.5 percentile

# All simulations
plot(bootstrap_sims$psve, 
     type="l", 
     main='PSVE for all simulations', 
     xlab='Simulation #', 
     ylab='PSVE')
abline(v = 0.025*nsim, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 2.5 percentile
abline(v = nsim/2, col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # median
abline(v = 0.975*nsim, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 97.5 percentile

# Num and denom in low-PSVE simulations
plot(bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$participant_id.TMS_Type, 
     type="o", 
     main=sprintf('TMS:participant variance (numerator) of simulations in bottom %d%% PSVE', bottom_percentile), 
     xlab='Simulation #', 
     ylab='Value')
abline(v = 0.025*nsim, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 2.5 percentile

plot(bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$sum, 
     type="o", 
     main=sprintf('Sum of variances (denominator) of simulations in bottom %d%% PSVE', bottom_percentile), 
     xlab='Simulation #', 
     ylab='Value')
abline(v = 0.025*nsim, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 2.5 percentile

# Component variances in low-PSVE simulations
all_y_values <- c(
  bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$participant_id.TMS_Type, 
  bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$participant_id, 
  bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$problem_id)
y_range <- range(all_y_values)

plot(bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$participant_id.TMS_Type, 
     type="o", 
     ylim=y_range, 
     main=sprintf('Variance components of simulations in bottom %d%% PSVE', bottom_percentile), 
     xlab='Simulation #', 
     ylab='Value')
lines(bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$participant_id, type="o")
lines(bootstrap_sims[1:bottom_percentile_idx,]$problem_id, type="o")
abline(v = 0.025*nsim, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) # 2.5 percentile

Test other model candidates

For completeness, we will try the likelihood ratio test with all low-AIC model formulas.

best_AIC <- correctness_models_da[order(correctness_models_da$AIC),]$AIC[[1]]
best_AIC
## [1] 472.2529
AIC_candidates <- correctness_models_da[correctness_models_da$AIC < best_AIC + 3,]
AIC_candidates <- AIC_candidates[order(AIC_candidates$AIC),]
AIC_candidates
## # A tibble: 3 × 3
##   formula                                                              AIC   BIC
##   <chr>                                                              <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) +…  472.  498.
## 2 correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) +…  474.  504.
## 3 correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) +…  474.  504.
# formulas with TMS fixed effect
has_TMS <- grepl('TMS_Type ', AIC_candidates$formula, fixed = TRUE)

add_fixed_TMS <- paste0(AIC_candidates$formula, ' + TMS_Type')
AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_full <- ifelse(
  has_TMS, AIC_candidates$formula, add_fixed_TMS)

AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced <- str_remove_all(
  AIC_candidates$formula, fixed('TMS_Type + '))
AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced <- str_remove_all(
  AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced, fixed('TMS_Type * '))

AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced <- str_remove_all(
  AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced, fixed(' + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type)'))
AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced <- str_remove_all(
  AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced, fixed(' + (1|problem_id:TMS_Type)'))
# unsure whether (1|participant_id:TMS_Type) should be removed in the reduced version
# debugging
for (f in AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_full){
  cat(f, '\n')
}
## correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type) 
## correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type) 
## correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1|participant_id:TMS_Type) + (1|problem_id:TMS_Type)
cat('\n\n')
for (f in AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced){
  cat(f, '\n')
}
## correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) 
## correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) 
## correctness ~ coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id)
AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_pval <- -1.0
for (i in seq_len(nrow(AIC_candidates))) {
  m_full <- glmer(
    AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_full[i],
    data = data,
    family = binomial
  )
  m_reduced <- glmer(
    AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_reduced[i],
    data = data,
    family = binomial
  )
  a <- anova(m_full, m_reduced)
  p_val <- a$"Pr(>Chisq)"[2]
  #print(p_val)
  AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_pval[i] <- p_val
}
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
AIC_candidates[AIC_candidates$fixed_TMS_pval < 0.05,]
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   formula              AIC   BIC fixed_TMS_full fixed_TMS_reduced fixed_TMS_pval
##   <chr>              <dbl> <dbl> <chr>          <chr>                      <dbl>
## 1 correctness ~ TMS…  472.  498. correctness ~… correctness ~ co…        0.00390
## 2 correctness ~ TMS…  474.  504. correctness ~… correctness ~ co…        0.0112 
## 3 correctness ~ TMS…  474.  504. correctness ~… correctness ~ co…        0.0112

This turns out to be more involved, and there are only 2 besides the original one so I’ll do it manually.

# 1. formula with fixed interaction
# a) test random interaction - significant
f_full_tmp <- AIC_candidates$formula[[2]]
f_red_tmp <- 'correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id)'

m_full_tmp <- glmer(
  f_full_tmp,
  data = data,
  family = binomial
)
m_reduced_tmp <- glmer(
  f_red_tmp,
  data = data,
  family = binomial
)
anova(m_full_tmp, m_reduced_tmp)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m_reduced_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id)
## m_full_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##               npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)   
## m_reduced_tmp    6 482.32 507.99 -235.16   470.32                        
## m_full_tmp       7 474.24 504.19 -230.12   460.24 10.076  1   0.001502 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
cat('\n\n')
# b) test fixed interaction - not significant
f_red_tmp <- 'correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)'
m_reduced_tmp <- glmer(
  f_red_tmp,
  data = data,
  family = binomial
)
anova(m_full_tmp, m_reduced_tmp)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m_reduced_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
## m_full_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type * coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
##               npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
## m_reduced_tmp    6 472.25 497.92 -230.13   460.25                     
## m_full_tmp       7 474.24 504.19 -230.12   460.24 0.0097  1     0.9213
# c) test marginal
# same as original tests (best-AIC model)
# 2. formula with additional random effect - not significant
f_full_tmp <- AIC_candidates$formula[[3]]
f_red_tmp <- 'correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1|participant_id) + (1|problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)'

m_full_tmp <- glmer(
  f_full_tmp,
  data = data,
  family = binomial
)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
m_reduced_tmp <- glmer(
  f_red_tmp,
  data = data,
  family = binomial
)
anova(m_full_tmp, m_reduced_tmp)
## Data: data
## Models:
## m_reduced_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type)
## m_full_tmp: correctness ~ TMS_Type + coding_section_no + (1 | participant_id) + (1 | problem_id) + (1 | participant_id:TMS_Type) + (1 | problem_id:TMS_Type)
##               npar    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
## m_reduced_tmp    6 472.25 497.92 -230.13   460.25                    
## m_full_tmp       7 474.25 504.20 -230.13   460.25     0  1          1
